bins.name: 200 MW data-center-only threshold
bins.desc: The filer argues the ANOPR's 20 MW threshold is far too low and would drag in manufacturers, hospitals, and other non-data-center loads. They propose a 200 MW floor applied only to data center facilities, citing industry data showing that typical modern facilities already exceed that level. A lower threshold would clog the interconnection queue and undermine the ANOPR's own efficiency goals.
quotes: The Commission should limit the scope of any rule to data center facilities 200 MW or greater.
A 20 MW threshold would sweep in numerous load customers for which the ANOPR was not designed.

any rule adopted by the Commission should be limited to data center facilities, i.e. facilities that primarily utilize energy-consuming information equipment, as opposed to other types of large load customers.

A 20 MW threshold would sweep in a multitude of load customers with a highly diverse set of characteristics, including manufacturing facilities, commercial businesses, and municipal load-serving entities.

bins.name: Rolled-in rates as default
bins.desc: The filer strongly supports rolled-in cost allocation as the default for network upgrades triggered by large load interconnections, citing longstanding Commission precedent. They argue that direct assignment of 100% of costs would provoke years of cost-allocation litigation, raise jurisdictional problems, and ultimately increase costs for other retail customers. Utilities and data centers are already developing bilateral agreements that protect ratepayers without mandating direct assignment.
quotes: Costs of transmission network upgrades triggered by interconnection of large load customers should, by default, be rolled into transmission rates consistent with longstanding Commission precedent on the treatment of integrated transmission facilities, rather than directly assigned to individual customers.

Rolled-in rate treatment will promote more efficient and timely large load interconnection by avoiding cost allocation and jurisdictional disputes, as well as ensuring that all load customers continue to contribute their fair share of the benefits of an integrated transmission network.
the Commission should bolster those efforts with an even-handed, across-the-board wholesale cost allocation to give the states a clean slate upon which to make retail rate determinations.
regardless of how the Commission decides to allocate the costs of network upgrades, it must consider how the effect of its action or inaction affects all customers—not just large load customers.

a federal requirement to assign 100 percent of transmission network upgrade costs to a particular end-use retail customer (the large load customer) . . . would eliminate the states' traditional authority to regulate how the utility allocates its total transmission costs among various retail customer rate classes and would be beyond the authority granted to the Commission by the United States Congress.

the Commission should use that jurisdiction to require that costs benefiting the transmission network be rolled into the transmission providers' rate base and recovered, with a reasonable rate of return, from all network transmission customers.

bins.name: Transmission owner return on upgrades
bins.desc: The filer insists that transmission owners must earn a reasonable rate of return on all network upgrade assets, citing Hope, Bluefield, and Ameren. Placing such assets in rate base at zero return would chill capital investment, create asymmetric risk, and violate constitutional requirements. As data-center-driven load growth accelerates, the volume of uncompensated upgrades would become unsustainable.
quotes: Costs of transmission network upgrades triggered by interconnection of large load customers should, by default, be rolled into transmission rates consistent with longstanding Commission precedent on the treatment of integrated transmission facilities, rather than directly assigned to individual customers.

Rolled-in rate treatment will promote more efficient and timely large load interconnection by avoiding cost allocation and jurisdictional disputes, as well as ensuring that all load customers continue to contribute their fair share of the benefits of an integrated transmission network.

it would be contrary to law and practically untenable for transmission owners to construct, operate, and maintain these facilities, while taking on the associated risks, without the facilities going into the transmission owners' rate base on which they are allowed to earn a reasonable return.
Under Hope, rates must, at a minimum, 'enable the company to operate successfully, to maintain its financial integrity, to attract capital, and to compensate its investors for the risks assumed.'

the Commission should use that jurisdiction to require that costs benefiting the transmission network be rolled into the transmission providers' rate base and recovered, with a reasonable rate of return, from all network transmission customers.

bins.name: State authority over retail cost allocation
bins.desc: The filer argues that requiring 100% direct assignment of transmission costs to a specific retail customer exceeds FERC's authority and encroaches on state jurisdiction over retail rate design. They urge FERC to limit its action to setting wholesale transmission rates and leave retail allocation to state regulators, consistent with New York v. FERC and FPA Section 824(b).
quotes: Rolled-in rate treatment will promote more efficient and timely large load interconnection by avoiding cost allocation and jurisdictional disputes, as well as ensuring that all load customers continue to contribute their fair share of the benefits of an integrated transmission network.
the Commission should bolster those efforts with an even-handed, across-the-board wholesale cost allocation to give the states a clean slate upon which to make retail rate determinations.

a federal requirement to assign 100 percent of transmission network upgrade costs to a particular end-use retail customer (the large load customer) . . . would eliminate the states' traditional authority to regulate how the utility allocates its total transmission costs among various retail customer rate classes and would be beyond the authority granted to the Commission by the United States Congress.

the Commission should use that jurisdiction to require that costs benefiting the transmission network be rolled into the transmission providers' rate base and recovered, with a reasonable rate of return, from all network transmission customers.

bins.name: Study deposits and readiness requirements
bins.desc: The filer endorses the ANOPR's proposal for study deposits and readiness requirements, viewing them as a practical check on speculative and duplicative interconnection requests that inflate queue length and delay processing.
quotes: The Indicated PJM TOs agree with principle 4 of the ANOPR proposal to the extent that it requires reasonable study deposits and readiness requirements for large load interconnections. These measures will help ensure that large load customers are sufficiently invested in the load interconnection process, which will deter speculative or duplicative requests.

bins.name: Separate study of load and generation in hybrids
bins.desc: The filer accepts coordinated study of hybrid facilities but requires that load and generation be studied independently as well, so the grid can reliably serve each component if the other is unavailable. They cite PJM's existing hybrid interconnection proposal as a workable model.
quotes: load and generation should be studied separately in the event the system needs to accommodate the full amount of the on-site load, or on-site generation, without the other.

bins.name: Queue priority over compressed timelines
bins.desc: The filer is skeptical of mandated shorter study periods for curtailable customers, warning that reduced timelines could be gamed with trivial curtailment commitments and could create reliability gaps. They prefer study queue priority conditioned on binding, meaningful curtailment commitments.
quotes: Rather than mandating a shorter study period for curtailable customers, the Commission could instead consider providing a study priority for curtailable large load customers relative to other large load customers, but limited to those large load customers that make a binding and enforceable commitment to offer a meaningful level of curtailment.

bins.name: Rigorous separate study process
bins.desc: The filer wants interconnection studies to remain thorough and distinct for load customers, with deposits and readiness checks to gate participation. Hybrid facilities need independent load and generation studies. For curtailable customers, study queue priority is preferable to mandated shorter periods.
quotes: The Indicated PJM TOs agree with principle 4 of the ANOPR proposal to the extent that it requires reasonable study deposits and readiness requirements for large load interconnections. These measures will help ensure that large load customers are sufficiently invested in the load interconnection process, which will deter speculative or duplicative requests.
load and generation should be studied separately in the event the system needs to accommodate the full amount of the on-site load, or on-site generation, without the other.

Rather than mandating a shorter study period for curtailable customers, the Commission could instead consider providing a study priority for curtailable large load customers relative to other large load customers, but limited to those large load customers that make a binding and enforceable commitment to offer a meaningful level of curtailment.

bins.name: Curtailment priority conditioned on firm commitment
bins.desc: The filer accepts the concept of expedited treatment for curtailable large load customers but opposes a blanket mandate for shorter study timelines. Any study priority must be tied to binding curtailment obligations at meaningful levels, not token commitments designed to game the system.
quotes: Rather than mandating a shorter study period for curtailable customers, the Commission could instead consider providing a study priority for curtailable large load customers relative to other large load customers, but limited to those large load customers that make a binding and enforceable commitment to offer a meaningful level of curtailment.

bins.name: Low threshold clogs the queue
bins.desc: The filer draws on PJM's experience with data-center-alley growth to warn that a 20 MW threshold would create enormous, mixed-type queues that slow processing for everyone. A narrower, higher threshold keeps the process tractable.
quotes: A 20 MW threshold would sweep in a multitude of load customers with a highly diverse set of characteristics, including manufacturing facilities, commercial businesses, and municipal load-serving entities.

bins.name: All-customer cost impact
bins.desc: The filer argues that cost allocation decisions must account for their impact on all retail customers. They note that rolled-in rates can actually lower costs for existing customers because large loads dilute the per-unit share, while direct assignment risks balkanizing the network and raising costs for non-data-center customers.
quotes: regardless of how the Commission decides to allocate the costs of network upgrades, it must consider how the effect of its action or inaction affects all customers—not just large load customers.

bins.name: Constitutional right to earn a return
bins.desc: Citing Hope, Bluefield, and the D.C. Circuit's Ameren decision, the filer argues that the regulatory compact requires utilities to earn a return commensurate with the risk of owning and operating network upgrades. Forcing transmission owners to hold growing volumes of zero-return assets would impair their financial integrity and deter the capital investment needed to meet AI-driven load growth.
quotes: it would be contrary to law and practically untenable for transmission owners to construct, operate, and maintain these facilities, while taking on the associated risks, without the facilities going into the transmission owners' rate base on which they are allowed to earn a reasonable return.
Under Hope, rates must, at a minimum, 'enable the company to operate successfully, to maintain its financial integrity, to attract capital, and to compensate its investors for the risks assumed.'

bins.name: No option to build for load customers
bins.desc: The filer opposes any option for large load customers to construct transmission upgrades. Load customers lack the technical qualifications, NERC reliability obligations, and operational expertise that utilities carry. Extending a build option would likely delay rather than accelerate interconnection, divert scarce engineering talent, and introduce reliability risks.
quotes: Large load customers should not have an option to build transmission upgrades.

Large load customers are not electric utilities, and do not possess the expertise and knowledge associated with developing and constructing transmission upgrades. Nor do they have a legal obligation to ensure reliability.

bins.name: APA rulemaking timeline concerns
bins.desc: The filer flags that the Secretary's April 30, 2026 deadline may not allow enough time for a legally sufficient NOPR and meaningful comment period. If the timeline cannot accommodate full APA procedures, it must be extended rather than truncated.
quotes: if this time limit is inconsistent with the full rulemaking process, it is not 'reasonable' within the meaning of 42 U.S.C. § 7173(b) and would need to be extended to accommodate those requirements.
